concepts.title: Torque about an axis
concepts.conceptBody:
Torque about an axis

Torque about an axis $\text{AB}$ is given by ${{\vec{\tau }}_{\text{AB}}}=\left( {{{\vec{\tau }}}_{0}}\cdot {{\overset{}{\mathop{n}}\,}_{\text{AB}}} \right)$ where $\text{O}$ is any point on axis $\text{AB}$. Thus, to find torque about an axis, find the torque about any point on the axis and take its projection along the axis.

Result : Torque (or angular momentum) found about an axis by choosing a point on the axis and taking its projection along the axis, is independent of the choice of the point on the axis.

concepts.title: Torque in various cases
concepts.conceptBody:
Torque in various cases

(i) When forces and axis are parallel $\left( \vec{F}//\text{AB} \right)$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \vec{\tau }=\left( \vec{r}\times \vec{F} \right) \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {{{\vec{\tau }}}_{\text{AB}}}=\left( \vec{r}\times \vec{F} \right)\cdot {{\overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,}_{\text{AB}}} \\ {} & {{{\vec{\tau }}}_{\text{AB}}}=0 \\ \end{array}$

(ii) When force and axis are coplanar and intersecting

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {\vec{\tau }} & ~=\left( \vec{r}\times \vec{F} \right) \\ {{{\vec{\tau }}}_{\text{AB}}} & ~=\left( \vec{r}\times \vec{F} \right)\cdot {{\overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,}_{\text{AB}}} \\ {} & ~=0 \\ \end{array}$

(iii) When force and axis directions are skew lines : (non-coplanar and non intersecting)

$\left( {{{\vec{\tau }}}_{\text{AB}}}\ne 0 \right)$

Tip : Torque about any axis can be visualized as a scalar triple product of $\vec{r},\vec{F}$ and ${{\overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,}_{\text{AB}}}$
${{\vec{\tau }}_{\text{AB}}}=\left( \vec{r}\times \vec{F} \right)\cdot {{\overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,}_{\text{AB}}}=\left[ \vec{r} \vec{F}{{\overset{}{\mathop{\text{n}}}\,}_{\text{AB}}} \right]$

$\left( \frac{\text{d}\vec{L}}{\text{dt}} \right)=\left[ \left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}\times {{{\vec{F}}}_{1}} \right)+\left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}\times {{{\vec{F}}}_{2}} \right) \right]+\left[ {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}\times {{{\vec{F}}}_{1,2}}+{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}\times {{{\vec{F}}}_{2,1}} \right]$

$={{\vec{\tau }}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}+\left[ {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}\times {{{\vec{F}}}_{1,2}}+{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}}\times -{{{\vec{F}}}_{1,2}} \right]$

$={{\vec{\tau }}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}+\left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{1}}-{{{\vec{r}}}_{2}} \right)\times {{\vec{F}}_{1,2}}$

$={{\vec{\tau }}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}+\left( {{{\vec{r}}}_{2,1}}\times {{{\vec{F}}}_{1,2}} \right)$

$={{\vec{\tau }}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}+0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \because {{{\vec{r}}}_{2,1}} \right.$ is anti parallel to $\left. {{{\vec{F}}}_{1,2}} \right]$

concepts.title: Moment and Couple
concepts.conceptBody:
Moment and Couple 

The moment of a force $\text{F}$ about a point is its turning effect about the point.

It is quantified as the product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the point to the line of action of force

In Figure the moment of $\text{F}$ about point $\text{O}$ is

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ moment }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{Fd}$

A couple is a pair of equal and parallel but opposite forces as shown in Figure:

The moment of a couple about any point in its plane is the product of one force and the perpendicular distance between them:

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Moment }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ couple }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{Fp}$

Example of a couple include turning on/off a tap, or winding a clock.

The SI units for a moment or a couple are Newton metres, Nm.

concepts.title: Work Done by a Constant Torque
concepts.conceptBody:
Work Done by a Constant Torque

Let a force $\text{F}$ turn a light rod OA with length $\text{r}$ through an angle of $\theta $ to position OB, as shown in Figure.

The torque ${{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}}$ exerted about $\text{O}$ is force times perpendicular distance from $\text{O}$.

${{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}}=\text{Fr}$

Now work done by $\text{F}$ is

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ work }\!\!~\!\!\text{ done }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{Fs}$

$\text{s}$ is the are of the circle, when $\theta $ is measure in radians

$\begin{matrix} \text{s}=\text{r}\theta \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ work }\!\!~\!\!\text{ done }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{Fr}\theta \\ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ work }\!\!~\!\!\text{ done }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }={{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}}\theta \\ \end{matrix}$

The work done by a constant torque ${{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}}$ is thus the product of the torque and the angle through which it turns (where the angle is measured in radians.)

As the SI units for work is Joules, ${{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}}$ must be in $\text{Nm}$

concepts.title: Power transmitted by a constant torque
concepts.conceptBody:
Power transmitted by a Constant Torque

Power is rate of doing work. If a rod rotates at $\text{n}$ revolutions per second, then in one second the angle turned through is

$\theta =2\pi \text{n}$ radians, and the work done per second will be,

$\text{ work }\text{ done }\text{ per }\text{ second }=\text{ power }={{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}}2\pi \text{n}$

as angular speed is $\omega =2\pi n$ then

$\begin{array}{l} \text{ power }=2\pi \text{n}{{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}} \\ \text{ power }=\omega {{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}} \\ \end{array}$

The units of power are Watts, W, with $n$ in rev/s, in rad/s and ${{\text{T}}_{\text{Q}}}$ in $\text{Nm}$.

concepts.title: Work Done by a variable torque
concepts.conceptBody:
Work done by a variable Torque 

In practice the torque is often variable. In this case the work done cannot be calculated as done earlier, but must be found in a similar way to that used for a variable force (see earlier.)

The total work done for the angular displacement $\theta $ is the area under the torque/displacement graph.

For variable torque

Work done $=$ area under torque/angular displacement graph

As with variable forces, in general you must use special integration technique to obtain the area under a curve.
